segments.s044-g25: من is the first-person pronoun (subject). ایران is the direct object. نخواهم is the present/future negative form of خواستن ('to want') meaning 'I do not want / I will not want.' The paired negatives نه ... نه ... enumerate what is rejected: 'neither Khāvar (the East) nor China.' Word order places the verb before the following negative list for poetic effect, but grammatically the subject–object–verb relation remains.
segments.s044-g26: نه ... نه ... continues the negative enumeration: 'neither ... nor ...'. شاهی here means 'kingship / sovereignty.' گسترده is an adjective 'extensive, wide' modifying implied 'domain(s)'. روی زمین is a prepositional phrase 'on the face/surface of the earth' with روی functioning as 'upon / on.' The whole phrase lists things the speaker denies wanting.
segments.s044-g27: بزرگی is a noun 'greatness / grandeur.' که introduces a relative clause ('which/whose'), linking بزرگی to its outcome. فرجام او is 'its outcome/end' with او referring back to بزرگی. تیرگی‌ست is the predicate ('is darkness'); ـست is the copula (classical literary form of است).
segments.s044-g28: بر آن مهتری: بر here means 'over / concerning / because of' and آن points to 'that (aforementioned) preeminence' (مهتری). The second بر + بباید + گریست is a classical/perhaps poetic construction meaning 'one ought to weep over it' — بباید is the modal 'must/ought to' and گریست is the verb 'to weep / lament.'
segments.s044-g29: سپهر بلند is a compound phrase 'the lofty/heavenly sky.' ار is the literary conditional 'if.' کشد here is a verb meaning 'draw/pull' (poetic use); it takes زین تو ('this/your saddle') as its object, so the clause means 'if the high sky pulls you from your saddle.' Note: the provided lemma senses lack the exact sense 'to pull/drag,' so the appropriate sense is not available in the list.
segments.s044-g30: سرانجام means 'in the end / ultimately.' خشت 'mudbrick' is the noun serving as predicate; است is the copula 'is.' بالین تو is a possessive phrase 'your pillow' (بالین = pillow). The line states the inevitable outcome: ultimately a brick will be your pillow (i.e., you will sleep on a brick in death).
segments.s044-g31: مرا ... را is the dative/accusative clitic 'for me / to me' (here split in the gloss as م + را) marking possession/experiencer: 'for me.' تخت ایران is 'the throne of Iran.' اگر introduces the conditional 'if' and بود is the past-tense copula used in a counterfactual conditional 'were.' زیر here means 'under/beneath.' The whole phrase is a classical inverted order: 'If the throne of Iran were under me.'
segments.s044-g32: کنون 'now.' گشتم is the past/resultive of گشتن used as 'I became' (here 'I would have become / I would now be'). از ... از marks the objects of satiation 'from/with crown and with throne' (i.e., 'weary of crown and throne'). سیر is 'satiated / satisfied' functioning as predicate complement: 'I have become satiated.'
segments.s044-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. برداشت is a past (3rd sing.) narrative verb 'lifted/removed'. پرده is the direct object 'curtain'. ز پیش آفتاب is an ablative/locative phrase 'from/in front of the sun' (ز = 'from', پیش = 'before/in the presence of', آفتاب = 'sun'), placed after the object as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s044-g2: سپیده برآمد is the main clause 'the dawn arose' (برآمد = 'arose/appeared' in past). بپالود is a past narrative form with the historical preverb ب- + پالود ('dispensed/cleansed', here 'drove away' or 'dispelled') and takes خواب ('sleep') as its object: '(it) dispelled sleep.'
segments.s044-g3: This is an elliptical, inverted noun phrase: 'دو بیهوده را دل' = 'the heart of the two foolish ones' (را marking the definite/affected NP). بدان کار is an old-fashioned locative/dative 'to that deed' formed as ب + دان (here serving together as 'to/for that'), and گرم is a predicate adjective 'warm/ardent' meaning 'became eager' — so literally 'the heart of the two became warm to that work.'
segments.s044-g4: که introduces a result/reason clause 'so that/that'. دیده here means 'eye(s)'. بشویند is the present/subjunctive (3rd pl.) with the historical b- prefix used in classical narrative — after که it expresses the resulting action 'they wash' (here idiomatically 'shed tears'). هر دو = 'both' (هر + دو). ز indicates cause 'from/because of' and شرم is 'shame/modesty'.
segments.s044-g5: برفتند is past tense (3rd pl.) 'they went/left'. هر دو qualifies 'گرازان' as 'both (of the) boars' (هر = 'each/every' functioning with دو to mean 'both'). ز جای is an ablative phrase 'from the place'. The word order (verb — subject phrase — ablative) reflects classical narrative style.
segments.s044-g6: نهادند is the past (3rd pl.) of نهادن 'to place/set' used here idiomatically of directing or setting the head (i.e. 'they turned their heads'). سر is the object 'head'. سوی is a preposition 'toward', and پرده‌سرای is the destination 'curtain-chamber' or 'stage/scene'.
segments.s044-g7: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. از خیمه ایرج is a prepositional phrase 'from Irāj's tent' (از = 'from', خیمه = 'tent', ایرج = name). به ره بنگرید is the verb phrase 'looked toward the road' — بنگرید is a past/look verb with the b- prefix used in narrative; word order places the source phrase before the verb as is typical.
segments.s044-g8: پر از مهر is an adjectival phrase 'full of affection' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of', مهر = 'love/affection'). دل is the experiencer 'heart'. پیش ایشان is a locative 'before/in the presence of them' and دوید is past 'ran/hurried' — idiomatically 'his heart ran forward (toward them)', i.e. he was eager to go to them.
segments.s044-g81: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening meaning "thus he said." کاینت is an archaic/poetic contraction (effectively کاینِ است) meaning "this is" or "such is." سر here means "essence/secret/heart" and is linked to the following phrase آن نیاز ("that supplication") — the expected ezāfe (سرِ آن) is omitted for poetic/metrical reasons, so the nouns appear adjacent. The whole phrase = "Thus he said: 'This is the heart/secret of that plea.'"
segments.s044-g82: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning "that" (a complement to "the heart/secret is ..."). تاج نیاگان is a genitive phrase "the crown of the ancestors." بدو is the prepositional/dative clitic phrase "to him/unto him." گشت is the past form of گشتن meaning here "turned/was turned/returned" (intransitive/passive sense), and باز is the adverb "again." Word order is typical older Persian: subject (تاج نیاگان) + indirect object (بدو) + verb (گشت) + adverb (باز) = "that the ancestors' crown turned back to him."
segments.s044-g83: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb). The repeated particle خواه ... و خواه ... is a correlative pair meaning 'either ... or ...' (here introducing two alternatives). تاجش (تاج + ـش) is 'his crown' (possessive clitic ـش). The imperative verb 'ده' (to give) is present in the phrase as the command 'give' (second person); it governs تاجش. تخت is the alternative object 'throne.'
segments.s044-g84: شد is the past tense 'became/was made' (3rd person singular). آن is the demonstrative 'that.' سایه‌گستر is a compound adjective/noun 'shade‑spreading' (i.e. providing shade). نیازی is an adjectival/possessive formation from نیاز, here qualifying درخت — effectively 'the supplicatory / of‑supplication' — so نیازی درخت = 'tree of supplication' or 'supplicatory tree.' The word order is slightly marked (verb شد preceding the noun phrase), a poetic inversion meaning 'that shade‑spreading supplicatory tree came to be.'
segments.s044-g85: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/went away.' باز means 'again' (adverb). آن دو is the demonstrative plus numeral 'those two.' بیداد is used as a substantive/epithet meaning 'injustice/tyranny' (here pluralized notionally as two oppressive forces), and شوم is an adjective 'ill‑omened, accursed.' The phrase order places the verb before the noun phrase for poetic effect: 'they departed again, those two ill‑omened bēdād.'
segments.s044-g86: یکی ... و یکی ... is a distributive construction meaning 'one ... and one ... / one... the other...' سوی is the preposition 'toward' or 'to.' ترک = 'the Turks' and روم = 'Rome/Byzantium.' The simple coordination with و links the two directions: 'one toward the Turks and one toward Rome.'
segments.s044-g65: Word order is subject ('یکی' = 'someone') + direct object ('خنجر آبگون' = 'bluish dagger') + verb ('برکشید' in past 3rd sg). 'آبگون' post‑modifies 'خنجر' as an adjectival phrase 'bluish', and 'برکشید' is the past of 'برکشیدن' used here in the sense 'to draw (a weapon)'.
segments.s044-g66: 'سراپای او' is a genitive/possessive phrase 'his whole body' ('سراپا' = head‑to‑toe/entirely + pronoun 'او'). The clause 'چادر خون کشید' literally 'drew/dragged a cloak of blood' uses past 'کشید' to describe the resultive covering — idiomatically 'a cloak of blood spread over him.' The subject is implied from the previous clause, and 'چادر' here means 'cloak/veil.'
segments.s044-g67: Here 'بدان' functions as the prepositional/instrumental 'with that' (literally 'به + آن'); it is followed by stacked modifiers 'تیز' (sharp) and 'زهر‑آبگون' (poison‑colored) that qualify the noun 'خنجر' (dagger). The enclitic '-ش' on 'خنجر‌ش' marks possession ('his/its dagger'). This segment supplies the instrument and its description and links to the action in the next segment.
segments.s044-g68: 'همی‌کرد' is the imperfective/iterative past form indicating a continuing or repeated action ('kept doing' / 'was doing'). 'چاک' is a noun 'a gash' that functions as the object of 'کرد' (to make), and 'آن کیانی برش' is a modifying/genitive phrase 'on that Kayanian's side' where 'آن' = 'that', 'کیانی' = 'Kayanian/royal' and 'برش' denotes the wound/cut. Word order (verb + object + genitive complement) is typical poetic narrative.
segments.s044-g69: 'فرود آمد' is a compound past verb meaning 'came down' / 'fell' (simple past). It is followed by the prepositional phrase 'از پای سرو سهی' = 'from/at the foot of the cypress' where 'سهی' is a poetic epithet attached to 'سرو' (cypress). The verbal phrase precedes the locative phrase here, a common order in narrative lines.
segments.s044-g70: 'گسست' is a past intransitive verb 'was rent/broke/separated.' The noun phrase 'آن کمرگاه شاهنشهی' ('that imperial waist') follows the verb (verb + subject/order inversion for poetic effect) and functions as the patient/affected part. 'شاهنشهی' modifies 'کمرگاه' as 'royal/imperial.'
segments.s044-g71: 'روان خون' is a noun phrase where 'روان' is a participial/adjectival modifier meaning 'flowing' (i.e., 'flowing blood'). 'از آن چهره' is the source phrase 'from that face.' 'ارغوان' follows as a color adjective 'crimson/purplish,' presented appositively to describe the blood or appearance in poetic order.
segments.s044-g72: 'شد' is the past of 'شدن' used copularly/perfectively ('became' / 'was'); here it introduces the resulting state. The following noun phrase 'آن نامور شهریار جوان' ('that renowned young sovereign') is placed after the verb (verb + nominal), a poetic inversion; the construction reports the change of state affecting the named ruler (contextually implying he was struck down).
segments.s044-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'he heard'. تور is the proper name Tūr; از + برادر is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) brother'. چنین modifies the preceding clause ('thus/this way'). The order is typical poetic Persian: 'When Tūr heard thus from (his) brother...'.
segments.s044-g42: This is an idiomatic result-clause: به ابرو ... آورد چین literally 'brought a wrinkle to the brow'. به is directional 'to/toward', ابرو 'brow', ز 'from' (poetic), خشم 'anger' gives the cause, اندر is the locative 'in/on', آورد is the past 'brought/caused', and چین is 'wrinkle'. Together: 'he caused a wrinkle to appear on his brow from anger.'
segments.s044-g43: نیامدش is an archaic/perfective verbal form meaning 'did not befit/was not agreeable to him' with the enclitic -ش 'to him'. گفتار ایرج پسند is the noun phrase 'the speech/words of Irāj (which would be) pleasing'. The structure therefore means 'Iraj's words did not please him.'
segments.s044-g44: نبد is an archaic negative/existential 'there was not' (from نبـود). راستی is 'truth/honesty'. نزد او is a prepositional phrase 'in his sight / before him'. ارجمند is a predicate adjective 'esteemed/valued'. The clause reads literally 'There was no truth esteemed before him' → 'honesty was not esteemed in his sight.'
segments.s044-g45: This clause uses repeated directional به: 'به کرسی' (to the seat/throne) and 'به خشم اندر آورد پای' 'in anger he brought/placed his foot'. آورد is the past 'brought/put' with پای ('foot') as its object. اندر before آورد gives the locative/causal 'in/with' (here 'in anger'). The word order is poetic but functions as 'he placed his foot on the throne in anger.'
segments.s044-g46: همی‌گفت is an imperfective/habitual past 'kept saying' or 'was saying'. و is the coordinator 'and'. برجست is a past verb 'he rose/started up' (poetic verb meaning to rise/appear). هزمان is an adverb 'simultaneously/at the same time'. ز جای is 'from/of his place'. Together: 'he was saying and simultaneously rose from his place.'
segments.s044-g47: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' or 'each in turn'. برآمد is past 'rose' or 'stood up'. ز جای 'from their places'. نشست here is the verb 'sat' or 'took a seat' (نشست can be noun or verb; in this context it functions as a verb 'sat down' following برآمد). The sequence describes people rising from and sitting in their places, with the verbs in simple past.
segments.s044-g48: گرفت is the past 'took/seized'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. گران is an adjective here 'valuable/precious' modifying کرسی 'seat/throne'; زر follows to specify material 'gold' (i.e. 'golden throne'). به‌دست is an adverbial/complement 'into his hand' or 'held in hand'. The whole: 'he seized that precious golden throne in his hand.'
segments.s044-g17: چنین بخششی is a noun phrase 'such a giving/ gift' (چنین = 'such' modifying بخششی = 'gift, giving'). کان is an archaic/relativizing particle here linking that gift to the agent (the world-seeker) and is followed by the actor جهان‌جوی; کرد is the simple past of کردن meaning 'made/gave' (lit. 'did'). Word order is demonstrative/nominal + relative particle + agent + past verb.
segments.s044-g18: همه سوی is an adverbial phrase 'to every side / in all directions' (همه = 'all', سوی = 'toward'). کهتر پسر is a noun phrase 'the smaller/younger son' (کهتر used relatively = 'smaller/younger' modifying پسر = 'son'). روی‌کرد is the past compound 'turned his face/turned toward' (رویِ + کرد), so the clause says the younger son turned toward all sides.
segments.s044-g19: نه ... نه frames a correlative negation ('neither ... nor'). تاج کیان is the possessive/genitive 'the crown of the Kians' (کیان = the Kayanian kings). مانم is the present/past-stem form of ماندن used here 'remains / is left to me' (elliptically 'is mine/left to me'); اکنون = 'now' and نه گاه continues the negation 'nor (even) a moment' (گه/گاه = a time/instant). The clause thus negates possession/remaining of the crown now or even for a moment.
segments.s044-g20: Another pair in the same negative series: نه نام بزرگی = 'nor the name (fame) of greatness' (نام = 'name/reputation', بزرگی = 'greatness'); نه ایران سپاه = 'nor Iran's army' (ایران as the land/realm, سپاه = 'army'). The nouns are simply juxtaposed under the continuing negation.
segments.s044-g21: چو = temporal conjunction 'when'. از تور = 'from Tūr' (از + proper name), with تور as the agent/source of the speech. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (third person singular), and ایرج is the subject placed before the object (speak/order 'سخن'). The clause order is temporal + source + verb + subject + object in the poetic inversion.
segments.s044-g22: یکی پاک‌تر پاسخ is a noun phrase 'a somewhat/paler but here 'a single, purer answer' (یکی = 'one/certain', پاک‌تر = comparative 'purer/more upright', پاسخ = 'reply'). افگند is the past of افکندن 'to cast/throw' used idiomatically with پاسخ to mean 'uttered/gave (a reply)'. بن at the line end is an archaic/poetic particle whose ordinary dictionary senses (root/base) do not match this line and likely functions as a discourse/verse connector; it is not one of the main lexical content words here.
segments.s044-g23: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت here is the past 'said' with a poetic diction). کای is the vocative particle 'O', followed by the vocative phrase مهتر کام‌جو‌ی = 'chief, seeker of desire' (مهتر = 'chief/elder', کام‌جوِ/کام‌جوی = 'one who seeks desire/pleasure'), the latter formed with the adjectival/noun-building suffix -ی.
segments.s044-g24: اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. کام دل = 'the wish/desire of the heart' (کام = 'desire', دل = 'heart' in genitive relation). خواهی is the present/optative 'you wish/want' (second person), and آرام جوی is an imperative/advisory 'seek calmly' (آرام = 'calm/slow', جوی = root meaning 'seek/search' used here as the verb form 'seek'). The clause is a conditional instruction: if you want your heart's desire, look for it peacefully.
segments.s044-g57: بسنده کنم is a two-word verbal phrase where بسنده (an adjectival/adverbial root 'sufficient/being content') combines with the personal verb کنم ('I will/do') to mean 'I will retire/be content (and withdraw)'. زین is the poetic contraction of 'از این' ('from this') and governs جهان ('the world'); گوشه‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase 'into a corner/secluded spot' indicating destination. Word order is topical/fronting (زین جهان as the domain), with the verb phrase signaling the speaker's intended action.
segments.s044-g58: به کوشش is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'by/with effort'. فراز here functions adverbially ('up/on high, upward') and combines with the verb آورم ('I will bring') to form 'I will bring up' or 'I will gather/raise'. توشه‌ای is the indefinite object 'a provision' (توشه + -ای). آورم is a first-person present/subjunctive/optative form used to express intent ('I will bring').
segments.s044-g59: This is a rhetorical interrogative. به marks the purpose/target ('for' or 'over'); خون برادر is a genitive phrase 'the brother's blood' (i.e. bloodshed concerning the brother). چه is the question word 'what/why' placed before the verb phrase; بندی کمر is literally 'you bind/gird the waist' (بندی = you bind/gird; کمر = waist/loins). Overall: 'For what do you gird your loins in your brother's blood?'
segments.s044-g60: چه is again interrogative 'what'; سوزی is a nominalized form meaning 'burning/pain/ache' and serves as the predicate ('what burning'); دل is the affected organ 'heart'. پیر گشته is a participial phrase 'having become old / now old' (گشته = 'has become'), modifying پدر 'father'. The clause asks what torment afflicts the father's heart now that he is aged.
segments.s044-g61: This clause consists of two short past clauses: جهان خواستی یافتی places the object جهان ('the world') up front for emphasis, then two past verbs addressing 'you' (خواستی 'you wished', یافتی 'you obtained'). خون مریز is an emphatic prohibition: مریز is an imperative/subjunctive negative-form meaning 'do not spill' taking خون ('blood', here 'bloodshed') as its object.
segments.s044-g62: مکن is the simple negative imperative 'do not'. با جهاندار is a prepositional phrase 'with/against the world‑ruler' (جهاندار = sovereign/holder of the world) and یزدان is 'God'; ستیز is a deverbal/nominal form 'fighting/strife'. Together the construction mکن ... ستیز issues the command 'do not contend/war with God, the ruler of the world' (ستیز used as the action banned by مکن).
segments.s044-g63: سخن را uses را as the definite object marker ('the speech/words'). چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = 'he heard'). پاسخ نداد is a negated past clause 'he did not give an answer' (پاسخ = answer; نداد = 'did not give'). The order is typical classical Persian: temporal clause before main clause, object before verb.
segments.s044-g64: همان گفتن آمد is a compact clause where گفتن ('saying' or 'the saying') is the nominal subject and آمد ('came') the verb: 'the same saying came'. The second phrase همان سرد باد is a noun phrase 'that same cold wind' (همان = the same/that, سرد = cold, باد = wind). The line juxtaposes the repeated verbal response and a cold wind metaphorically.
segments.s044-g9: برفتند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb meaning 'they went/left' and is placed at the start for poetic emphasis. با او is a companion phrase 'with him' (با + pronoun او). به + خیمه درون = 'into the tent/inside the tent' where به means 'towards/into' and درون 'inside' follows the noun.
segments.s044-g10: سخن بیشتر بر is a topical phrase: سخن 'conversation/talk' modified by بیشتر 'more/mostly' and governed by the preposition بر 'upon/toward' to mark what the talk concerned. چرا رفت و چون is an interrogative cluster 'why he went and how' where رفت (past) has an implicit subject (referring back to the person just mentioned) and چون here functions as 'how'.
segments.s044-g11: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. تور is the vocative proper name 'Tūr'. ار introduces a conditional 'if', followed by تو 'you' and از ما 'of/from us' (meaning 'one of us'); کهی is an archaic/emphatic particle (literally 'sometimes' in the lexical list) used here as an interrogative/emphatic element meaning roughly 'at all/indeed'.
segments.s044-g12: چرا is the question word 'why'. برنهادی is a past (2nd‑person) form of برنهاد 'to place on/put upon' (here 'you placed/put on'), so the line asks why the addressee did (or did not) put on the item. کلاه مهی = 'the moon‑like cap' (کلاه 'hat/cap', مهی 'moon‑like/beautiful') with مهی qualifying کلاه.
segments.s044-g13: ترا (here written as ت + را) is an object/emphatic form 'you' placed before the modal باید for emphasis. باید is a modal 'must/ought' (here rhetorical: 'will you [do such a thing]'). ایران و تخت کیان is a coordinated noun phrase 'Iran and the throne of the Kians' serving as the thing being given up or handed over.
segments.s044-g14: مرا (here split as م + را) is the accusative 'me'. بر در = 'on/at the gate' (بر 'on/upon', در 'gate/door'). ترک 'Turk' specifies whose gate it is, and بسته is a past participle/adjective 'bound/tied'. میان 'between/among' here completes the phrase 'bound among the Turks' — the whole segment is a rhetorical question: 'and (will you) leave me bound at the Turks' gate?'
segments.s044-g15: برادر 'brother' begins the question; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. مهتر is a comparative/adjectival noun 'greater/distinguished'. به خاور 'in the East' and به رنج 'by toil/suffering' are prepositional phrases that modify مهتر (i.e. 'greater in the East through hardship').
segments.s044-g16: به سر بر ترا = 'on (to) your head': به 'to/for', سر 'head', بر 'on/upon', plus the 2nd‑person clitic ت and the object marker را making 'your'. افسر is 'helmet' (a crown/helm placed upon the head). و links the next phrase; زیر گنج = 'under (you) a treasure' (زیر 'under', گنج 'treasure'), i.e. 'and set a treasure beneath you.' The line is a rhetorical question about who would so honor you.
segments.s044-g73: جهانا is a vocative form addressing the world (“O world”). بپروردی‌ش is a past-tense verb + enclitic object: بپروردی (you reared/raised) + -ش (his/her/its), so “you reared him.” در کنار is a prepositional phrase “at/by the side (of)” with در (in/at) + کنار (side/beside) following the verb to indicate location.
segments.s044-g74: وز آن پس is a poetic sequence roughly = از آن پس (“after that”); وز here is an archaic/poetic preposition equivalent to از. ندادی is 2nd-person past negative (you did not give). به جان is a prepositional phrase “to/for life/soul” (here ‘life’), and زینهار is an interjection/admonition “zinehâr” = “beware/take heed.”
segments.s044-g75: نهانی is an adverb ‘secretly’/‘in private’. ندانم is present/future-subjunctive-looking classical form meaning “I do not know” (negated know). ترا is the object clitic ‘you’ (ī.e., ت + a), and را is the object/definite marker; دوست کی است is a verbless copular clause ‘who is (your) friend’ (کی = who, ست = is). Word order is emphatic: adverb + verb + object + predicative question.
segments.s044-g76: بدین آشکارت is a prepositional phrase ‘by/with this manifest/clear (thing)’; بدین functions as بِ + این (‘by this’), and آشکارت is آشکار ‘clear/manifest’ (here describing the thing). بباید is a modal ‘one must/it is necessary’ and گریست is the infinitive/gerundial verb ‘to weep’/‘to lament’ — together 'one must weep.'
segments.s044-g77: سر تاجور is a genitive phrase ‘the head of the merchant’ (سر = head + تاجور = merchant). ز آن تن is a prepositional phrase ‘from that body’ (ز = from — poetic variant of از — + آن = that + تن = body). پیلوار is an adjective modifying تن meaning ‘continuous/whole/successive,’ describing the body from which the head was removed.
segments.s044-g78: به خنجر جدا کرد is the verbal clause: به خنجر indicates the instrument ‘with a dagger’ (به ... as ‘by/with’), جدا کرد is a compound of جدا ‘apart’ + کرد (did/made) = ‘separated/severed.’ و برگشت کار links with و = ‘and,’ برگشت is a past action ‘returned/turned back,’ and کار here means ‘the matter/affair’ or simply functions to indicate the action’s completion — idiomatically ‘and then he turned away/left.’
segments.s044-g79: مغز‌ش is مغز ‘brain’ + -ش (his), i.e. ‘his brain.’ بیاگند here is a poetically used verb meaning to treat the brain (in context: to scent/prepare it) — the exact classical sense is idiomatic, but grammatically it is the verb governing the rest. به مشک و عبیر is an instrumental/with-phrase ‘with musk and ambergris’ (به = with/to + مشک = musk + و + عبیر = fragrance/ambergris).
segments.s044-g80: فرستاد is a simple past verb ‘he sent.’ نزد introduces the recipient ‘to/in the presence of’ and جهان‌بخش پیر is the compound title of the addressee: جهان‌بخش ‘world-bestowing/benefactor of the world’ + پیر ‘old/elder (here: venerable).’ The phrase as a whole means he sent (it) to the venerable world-bestower.
segments.s044-g33: سپردم is a past first-person verb 'I entrusted/handed over.' شما را functions as the recipient ('to you') with the object-marker را attached (classical usage sometimes marks the indirect recipient with را). کلاه و نگین are the definite direct objects ('the crown and the jewel') following the verb in this poetic inversion.
segments.s044-g34: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase به + این ('by/with this' or 'in this matter'); روی here means 'manner/face/on account' ('in this way' or 'because of this'). با من = 'toward me / with me.' مدارید is a 2nd-person-plural present/subjunctive/jussive form from مداریدن/مدارا کردن ('be tolerant / do not treat harshly') used as an admonition; کین ('hatred/vengeance') is the object governed by مدارید: 'do not treat me with hatred.'
segments.s044-g35: مرا با شما is a compact way to say 'between me and you' (literally 'me with you'); here 'مرا' is the first participant marked with را and followed by با شما. نیست negates existence ('there is not'); ننگ و نبرد are the coordinated nouns negated by نیست: 'there is no shame and no fight between us.'
segments.s044-g36: روان را is the direct object 'the soul/spirit' (marked by را). نباید is an impersonal modal 'one should not / it must not.' برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این ('upon this / because of this'); رنجه کردن is a causative/affected construction ('to cause to suffer / to distress'), with کرد as the light verb completing the infinitival/oblique action: 'one should not cause the spirit to be distressed by this.'
segments.s044-g37: زمانه ('fortune / the times') is the topical subject here. نخواهم is first-person negative ('I will not' / 'I do not wish to'); together the clause expresses the speaker's refusal or wish that fate not harm the addressees. به آزار‌تان is a prepositional phrase marking the effect ('to your harm').
segments.s044-g38: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' دور مانم uses مانم (1st-person present/subjunctive of ماندن) 'I remain' or 'I should remain' in the protasis. ز is the poetic variant of از ('from / away from'), and دیدار‌تان is 'your sight / your meeting' (تان is the 2nd-person plural possessive). Together: 'if I stay far from seeing you.'
segments.s044-g39: جز از = 'except / apart from.' کهتری means 'humility / inferiority' here (the quality the speaker claims). نیست negates existence. آیین من ('my custom / practice') is the topical predicate; the line states that the speaker's practice consists only of humility: 'my custom is nothing but being humble.'
segments.s044-g40: مباد is an optative/negative jussive ('may it not be / let it not be'). The following nouns آز و گردن‌کشی are the undesirable qualities ('greed and defiance/pride'), and دین من ('my religion/creed') states the domain to which the optative applies: 'may greed and arrogance never be my religion/way.'
segments.s044-g49: بزد is a past/telic verb 'he struck/hit' used here as the main action. بر سر is the prepositional phrase 'upon the head' (preposition بر + noun سر). خسرو تاجدار is a noun phrase 'Khosrow, the crowned (one)' with تاجدار as an epithet following the name.
segments.s044-g50: از او خواست is 'he begged/asked of him' (از + pronoun او + past خواست used as a request). ایرج is the name of the supplicant and appears after the verb in the poetical word order. به جان is an oath-formula 'by (your) life' (به used adverbially 'by/-ly' + جان), and زینهار is an interjection/command 'beware' or 'take heed'.
segments.s044-g51: نیایدت is a poetical impersonal/modal form (نیاید + enclitic -ت 'to you') meaning 'would it not come to you' / 'ought you not to...' used to introduce an imagined utterance. گفت here stands for saying/speech (poetically placed), ایچ بیم از خدای is the quoted phrase 'no fear of God' (ایچ = 'nothing/no', بیم = 'fear', از خدای = 'of/from God'). The whole is a rhetorical question.
segments.s044-g52: نه opens the reply 'No' (negation/contrast). شرم از پدر خود is a noun phrase 'shame before your own father' (شرم = shame; از = before/of in poetic use; پدر خود = your own father). همین است رای is a copular clause 'this is the counsel/opinion' (همین = this, است = is, رای = counsel/opinion) stating that 'shame before your father' is the recommended course.
segments.s044-g53: مکش is the negative imperative 'do not (kill/strike)'. مر مرا is an emphatic object sequence meaning 'me' (old/poetic duplication for emphasis). را is the object marker. که‌ت is que/که with the enclitic -ت 'that/which you', introducing the clause that follows. سرانجام کار is the noun phrase 'the outcome/end of the matter' (سرانجام = outcome, کار = affair).
segments.s044-g54: بپیچاند (prefixed ب-) is a verb meaning 'will turn/wind/wrap up' or 'bring to a close' (third-person singular, future/implicative sense in poetry). از خون من is an ablative/means phrase 'with/from my blood'. کردگار (the Creator/God) is the agent and follows the verb in inverted poetic order: 'the Creator will bring the affair to an end by my blood.'
segments.s044-g55: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. خویشتن را is the emphatic object 'yourself' (خویشتن = self, را = object marker). ز مردم‌کشان uses the poetic preposition ز 'from/of' plus the compound مردم‌کشان 'people-slayer' (literally 'killer of people') to mean 'become a slayer of people' — here: 'do not make yourself into a slayer of men.'
segments.s044-g56: کزین پس is a temporal phrase 'after this/ henceforth' (کزین = from this, پس = after/then). نیابی is the (negative) verbal form 'you will not find' (یاب- 'find'). ز من خود نشان means 'a trace/sign of me' (ز = of/from, من = me, خود = myself, نشان = sign/trace). Together: 'After this you will find no trace of me.'

sentences.s044-s13: I do not want Iran — neither the East nor China; I desire neither kingship nor wide dominion on the face of the earth.
sentences.s044-s14: If greatness's outcome is darkness, one ought to weep for that preeminence.
sentences.s044-s15: If the lofty heavens pull you from this saddle, in the end a brick will be your pillow.
sentences.s044-s16: If the throne of Iran were beneath me, I would now have grown weary of crown and throne.
sentences.s044-s1: When he lifted the curtain before the sun, the dawn rose and chased away sleep.
sentences.s044-s2: The hearts of the two foolish ones were stirred to that deed, so that both their eyes were washed from shame.
sentences.s044-s3: Both the boars departed from their place and turned their heads toward the curtain-chamber.
sentences.s044-s4: When Irāj looked out from his tent toward the road, his heart, full of affection, ran forward before them.
sentences.s044-s41: Thus he said: "This is the heart of that supplication — that the ancestors' crown returned to him."
sentences.s044-s42: Now — either give him his crown or give him the throne — that shade‑spreading tree of supplication came into being.
sentences.s044-s43: Those two ill‑omened injustices/tyrants departed again; one went toward the Turks and the other toward Rome.
sentences.s044-s33: Someone drew a bluish dagger; a cloak of blood spread over him from head to foot.
sentences.s044-s34: With that sharp, poison‑tinged dagger he kept making a gash in the Kayanian's side.
sentences.s044-s35: He fell at the foot of the dark cypress; the imperial waist was torn apart.
sentences.s044-s36: Blood flowed from that face, crimson; the famed young sovereign was struck down.
sentences.s044-s21: When Tūr heard this from his brother, he drew a wrinkle on his brow from anger.
sentences.s044-s22: Iraj's words did not please him; honesty was not esteemed in his sight.
sentences.s044-s23: He set his foot on the throne in anger; he kept speaking and at the same time rose up from his place.
sentences.s044-s24: One by one they rose from their places and sat; he seized that costly golden throne in his hand.
sentences.s044-s9: Such a gift as the world-seeker gave, the younger son turned his face in every direction.
sentences.s044-s10: I have neither the Kayanian crown now nor even a moment; neither great renown nor Iran's army.
sentences.s044-s11: When Irāj heard the words from Tūr, he cast forth a purer answer.
sentences.s044-s12: He said to him: 'O chief, seeker of desires, if you desire the wish of your heart, seek it calmly.'
sentences.s044-s29: I will withdraw from this world to a secluded corner, and with effort bring together a provision.
sentences.s044-s30: Why gird your loins for (your) brother's blood? What burning seizes the heart of the now‑aged father?
sentences.s044-s31: You desired the world and you obtained it; do not spill blood — do not wage war against God, the ruler of the world.
sentences.s044-s32: When he heard the words he gave no answer; the same saying came — the same cold wind.
sentences.s044-s5: They went with him into the tent, and the conversation then turned mainly to why and how he had gone.
sentences.s044-s6: He said to him, 'Tūr, if you are one of us, why did you not place that moon‑like cap?'
sentences.s044-s7: Will you hand over Iran and the throne of the Kians and leave me bound at the Turk‑gate?
sentences.s044-s8: What brother—one greater in the East through hardship—would place a helmet on your head and a treasure beneath you?
sentences.s044-s37: O world, you reared him at your side, and after that you did not give him life — beware!
sentences.s044-s38: I do not know (in secret) who your friend is; at this open revelation one must weep.
sentences.s044-s39: He severed the merchant’s head from that body with a dagger, and then turned away.
sentences.s044-s40: He sent his brain, scented with musk and ambergris, to the aged world-bestower.
sentences.s044-s17: I entrusted to you the crown and the jewel; on this account do not harbour hatred against me.
sentences.s044-s18: Between you and me there is neither shame nor battle; one should not let the spirit be distressed by this.
sentences.s044-s19: I will not let fortune harm you, if I remain distant from your sight.
sentences.s044-s20: My custom is nothing but humility; may greed and arrogance never be my creed.
sentences.s044-s25: He struck the crowned Khosrow on the head; Iraj begged him, 'By your life, beware!'
sentences.s044-s26: Would it not occur to you to say, 'Have you no fear of God?' — No: 'Shame before your father' — that is the counsel.
sentences.s044-s27: Do not slay me; for in the end the affair will be wound up with my blood by the Creator.
sentences.s044-s28: Do not make yourself a slayer of men; after this you will find no trace of me.
